real_meaning: ～ておく expresses doing something <ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>もって — in advance, or as preparation for something that's coming. The core feeling is deliberate forethought: you're doing something NOW so that things will be in a better, ready state LATER. It can also mean leaving something in a certain state on purpose (sometimes translated as 'leaving it as is'), where the 'preparation' is simply maintaining the status quo for a reason.

formation.rule: Attach ておく to the て-form of a verb. In casual speech, ておく contracts to とく (and ておいて → といて). Examples: <ruby>準備<rt>じゅんび</rt></ruby>する → <ruby>準備<rt>じゅんび</rt></ruby>しておく | <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>う → <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>っておく | <ruby>開<rt>あ</rt></ruby>ける → <ruby>開<rt>あ</rt></ruby>けておく (casual: <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>っとく、<ruby>開<rt>あ</rt></ruby>けとく)
formation.common_mistake: Learners sometimes attach おく directly to the dictionary form instead of the て-form. Wrong: <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>うおく ✗ — Correct: <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>っておく ✓

sentences.jp: <ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby>の<ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>に、<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>読<rt>よ</rt></ruby>んどいたほうがいいよ。
sentences.translation: You should read through the materials before tomorrow's meeting.
sentences.notes: Uses the casual contraction んどいた (= んでおいた), naturally emphasizing reading in advance as smart preparation.

sentences.jp: <ruby>旅行<rt>りょこう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>に、ホテルを<ruby>予約<rt>よやく</rt></ruby>しておきました。
sentences.translation: I made a hotel reservation in advance of the trip.
sentences.notes: The most classic use of ておく — completing an action ahead of time so a future situation is taken care of.

sentences.jp: ご<ruby>来場<rt>らいじょう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>に、<ruby>座席<rt>ざせき</rt></ruby>のご<ruby>予約<rt>よやく</rt></ruby>をしておいてください。
sentences.translation: Please make a seat reservation in advance before attending.
sentences.notes: ておく appears in formal written/spoken instructions to convey that the preparation step is expected of the listener.

sentences.jp: その<ruby>件<rt>けん</rt></ruby>はもう<ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>っておいたから、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>だよ。
sentences.translation: I've already told her about that, so it's fine.
sentences.notes: Here ておく implies 'I took care of it proactively so you don't have to worry' — it carries a reassuring, I-handled-it nuance beyond simple past tense.

confused_with.contrast: Both describe a state resulting from a deliberate action, but the focus differs. ～ておく focuses on the ACT of preparation — the speaker's intentional doing. ～てある focuses on the resulting STATE — something has been done and is now set up that way. Minimal pair: <ruby>窓<rt>まど</rt></ruby>を<ruby>開<rt>あ</rt></ruby>けておいた (I opened the window [as preparation / on purpose]) vs. <ruby>窓<rt>まど</rt></ruby>が<ruby>開<rt>あ</rt></ruby>けてある (The window is open [left open intentionally by someone]). Notice that ておく takes the object particle を and keeps the agent in focus, while てある shifts to が and foregrounds the resulting state. Use ておく when you want to emphasize the deliberate advance action; use てある when you want to describe the ready state itself.

confusion_set.use_when: <ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>もって<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>かをする — reach for this when the emphasis is on deliberately doing something NOW in preparation for later.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>パーティーの<rt></rt></ruby><ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>み<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>っておいた。

confusion_set.use_when: The action is already done and the resulting state is what matters — emphasizes the SET-UP state, not the actor.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>み<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>はもう<ruby>冷蔵庫<rt>れいぞうこ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れてある。

confusion_set.use_when: The action is completed fully — often with a nuance of finality, regret, or getting something out of the way (not advance preparation).
confusion_set.example: <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>を<ruby>全部<rt>ぜんぶ</rt></ruby>やってしまった。

bunpro_tip: Mental model: picture yourself as a <ruby>準備<rt>じゅんび</rt></ruby>の<ruby>達人<rt>たつじん</rt></ruby> — a prep master — placing something neatly in position before it's needed. Every time you see ておく, ask: 'What future situation is this action preparing for?' Also drill the casual contraction とく aggressively, since native input is full of it and BunPro sentences may use either form.
